mnemonics.title: Memory Read Steps
mnemonics.content: READ STEPS: ADDRESS PUT ON BUS, READ SIGNAL SENT, MEMORY FETCHES, DATA ON BUS, CPU READS DATA

mnemonics.title: Memory Write Steps
mnemonics.content: WRITE STEPS: ADDRESS AND DATA PUT ON BUS, WRITE SIGNAL SENT, MEMORY STORES DATA, ACKNOWLEDGMENT SENT

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations
mnemonics.content: PUSH POPS DOWN: PUSH DECREMENTS THEN STORES, POP RETRIEVES THEN INCREMENTS

mnemonics.title: Memory Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: SMALL FAST EXPENSIVE: Registers > Cache > RAM > Disk (size increases, speed decreases)

acronyms.usage: Main memory that can be read and written directly in constant time regardless of location
Non-volatile memory that retains data without power, used for firmware
Fast, expensive memory using flip-flops, used for cache memory
Main memory using capacitors, needs refresh, slower than SRAM but cheaper
Allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
Hardware unit that handles virtual memory translation and memory protection

visualTips.visualization: Think of a library system: You give the librarian an address (book location), they find and hand you the book (data) - CPU requests, Memory delivers
Think of placing an item in a storage locker: You specify the locker number, place your item inside, and the system records it - CPU sends address+data, Memory stores
Think of a stack of plates in a cafeteria: PUSH adds plate to top, POP removes from top - LIFO (Last In First Out) principle
Think of a refrigerator: Keep frequently used items (milk, butter) close at hand (cache), less frequently used items in the main storage (RAM)

analogies.analogy: Library analogy: Librarian's desk = registers (fastest), reading table = cache (quick access), bookshelves = main memory, warehouse = disk storage (slowest, largest)
Address bus is like a house address on an envelope (where to send), Data bus is like the letter inside (the actual content being transferred)
Like keeping frequently used tools on your workbench instead of going to the toolbox every time - saves trips to main storage
Like using a library's storage room: books not currently needed can be stored in the basement (disk), bringing them to the main area (RAM) when needed
